title: Seven Gifts from the Hollow
synopsis: A tiny field mouse named Pebble tells a small lie that grows bigger with each mysterious gift that arrives. Told by an ancient acorn cup, this folkloric tale weaves through treehouses and rope bridges as seven magical gifts reveal the truth about courage and honesty.

pages.text: The morning dew still clings to my rim when the sparrow brings the first package. I am an acorn cup, carved smooth by time and weather, and I have watched over this village of treehouses for seven seasons. The rope bridges sway gently in the dawn breeze, creaking their ancient song. Below, Pebble the field mouse scurries across the wooden planks, her bluebell-flower cap bobbing with each quick step. The package is no bigger than a walnut, wrapped in silver birch bark. 'For the bravest mouse in the village,' reads the tiny scroll tied with spider silk. Pebble's black eyes grow wide as midnight pools. Yesterday, she told the other mice she had chased away the great owl that lives in the dead oak. But I saw what truly happened - she hid beneath my shadow, trembling like a leaf in storm wind.

illustration.description: A tiny field mouse with soft tan fur, rounded ears, a pink nose, and bright shiny black eyes, wearing a bluebell-flower cap, holding a small package wrapped in silver bark. The scene shows treehouses connected by rope bridges in an enchanted forest village. Golden age illustration, Arthur Rackham inspired, detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale style with decorative borders.
illustration.alt: Pebble the mouse receives her first mysterious gift on a rope bridge

pages.text: By the second day, word of Pebble's supposed bravery has spread through every treehouse. The squirrels chatter about it from branch to branch. The second gift arrives with the afternoon rain - a tiny silver acorn that gleams like starlight. Inside, Pebble finds a whistle carved from bone. The third gift comes at sunset, carried by a beetle wearing a crown of moss. It contains seeds that shimmer blue and green, like captured pieces of sky and forest. 'For she who protects us all,' says the note. I watch Pebble's whiskers twitch with worry. The other mice gather around her now, asking when she will face the owl again. Her small paws shake as she tucks the gifts away. The lie grows heavier with each present, like stones filling a pouch.

illustration.description: A tiny field mouse with soft tan fur, rounded ears, a pink nose, and bright shiny black eyes, wearing a bluebell-flower cap, surrounded by various small wrapped gifts and a beetle messenger. The treehouse village glows in sunset light through the background. Golden age illustration, Arthur Rackham inspired, detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale style with decorative borders.
illustration.alt: Pebble examines magical gifts while other forest creatures watch expectantly

pages.text: Gifts four and five arrive together on the third morning, brought by twin butterflies whose wings whisper secrets. A cloak of woven grass that changes color with the wearer's mood, and a compass that points not north, but toward one's greatest fear. Pebble's cloak turns the color of storm clouds. The sixth gift comes with the evening meal - a cup like me, but made of crystal that shows true reflections. When Pebble looks into it, she sees herself as she really is: small, frightened, but with a spark of something bright burning in her heart. The mice have begun to sing songs about her courage. They speak of building a statue in her honor. But I see how she startles at every shadow, how she takes the longest path to avoid the dead oak where the great owl sleeps.

illustration.description: A tiny field mouse with soft tan fur, rounded ears, a pink nose, and bright shiny black eyes, wearing a bluebell-flower cap, examining a crystal cup that shows her reflection. She wears a color-changing cloak and various magical items surround her in a cozy treehouse chamber. Golden age illustration, Arthur Rackham inspired, detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale style with decorative borders.
illustration.alt: Pebble looks into a magical crystal cup that reveals truth

pages.text: On the seventh day, as the village prepares for the Feast of Brave Hearts in Pebble's honor, the final gift arrives. It comes with no messenger, simply appearing beside me at dawn like morning mist made solid. Inside the last package lies a mirror no bigger than a dewdrop, and with it, a note that makes my ancient wood grain shiver: 'The owl knows the truth. Face what you fear, or lose what you love.' Through the rope bridges, I hear the great owl's call echoing from the dead oak. It speaks in the old language that only the wind and I remember: 'Little mouse, your courage is not in your size, but in your honesty. Come speak truth, and find what you seek.' Pebble clutches all seven gifts to her tiny chest. The village mice gather on the bridges, watching, waiting for their hero to prove herself once more.

illustration.description: A tiny field mouse with soft tan fur, rounded ears, a pink nose, and bright shiny black eyes, wearing a bluebell-flower cap, standing at the edge of rope bridges facing a massive dead oak tree where a great owl's silhouette looms. All seven magical gifts float around her in a mystical arrangement. Golden age illustration, Arthur Rackham inspired, detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale style with decorative borders.
illustration.alt: Pebble faces the great owl's tree with all seven magical gifts surrounding her

pages.text: The rope bridges hold their breath as Pebble crosses toward the dead oak. I roll slightly on my branch to see better. When she reaches the owl's tree, her voice is small but clear: 'I told a lie. I hid instead of chasing you away. But I want to be brave for real now.' The great owl descends, larger than three mice together, but his eyes are kind as amber honey. 'Little mouse,' he hoots softly, 'the gifts were never for your false courage. They were for the courage it takes to speak truth.' He gestures with one great wing toward the village. 'I sent them because I saw you save a baby mouse from falling, saw you share your winter stores with the hungry voles, saw you sing lullabies to the frightened younglings during storms. Real bravery is not the absence of fear - it is kindness in the face of fear.' The seven gifts begin to glow, and I understand at last why they came. Not for the lie, but for the truth that lived beneath it all along.

illustration.description: A tiny field mouse with soft tan fur, rounded ears, a pink nose, and bright shiny black eyes, wearing a bluebell-flower cap, speaking with a magnificent great owl in a dead oak tree. The seven magical gifts glow with ethereal light around them as other mice watch from rope bridges in the background. Golden age illustration, Arthur Rackham inspired, detailed pen work, watercolor washes, ornate fairy tale style with decorative borders.
illustration.alt: Pebble and the wise owl share a moment of understanding as the magical gifts glow

questions.question: What did Pebble tell the other mice she had done?
questions.explanation: Pebble lied and said she chased away the great owl, but really she hid beneath the acorn cup's shadow.

questions.question: How many gifts did Pebble receive?
questions.explanation: Pebble received seven magical gifts over seven days, each one revealing something about true courage.

questions.question: What happened to Pebble's cloak when she felt scared?
questions.explanation: The magical cloak changed color with the wearer's mood, turning storm cloud gray when Pebble felt frightened.

questions.question: Who was telling this story?
questions.explanation: The story is narrated by an ancient acorn cup that watches over the village of treehouses.

questions.question: Why did the owl really send the gifts?
choices: For her real acts of kindness
questions.explanation: The owl sent gifts because he saw Pebble's true courage in helping others and showing kindness despite her fears.
